text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Susan  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Susan,  

Anna Patel, DOB: 21-07-1992, Hospital No: U874512 NHS No. 1029384756  
4 Brookside Way, London, NW1 6AB 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Focal epilepsy with suspected catamenial pattern/previous concussion in adolescence/no learning disability/normal MRI 2023/mild generalised anxiety symptoms. 

Medication: Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily.  
Clobazam 10 mg at night (intermittent short courses around menses as advised).  
Folic acid 5 mg daily.  

I reviewed Anna in clinic today. She attended alone and was well-engaged with her care. She has maintained full-time employment as a graphic designer and has recently reduced caffeine intake. She reports good sleep hygiene, though her work occasionally involves early-morning deadlines. She drives and remains aware of DVLA guidance; there has been no loss of awareness events while driving. 

Seizures: She has focal epilepsy. Typical events begin with a rising epigastric sensation and brief déjà vu, followed by impaired awareness with lip-smacking and right-hand fumbling lasting about 60–90 seconds; recovery is within 10–15 minutes with residual fatigue. Frequency over the last six months has averaged 1–2 focal impaired-awareness episodes per month. She notes clusters tending to occur in the late luteal phase and first two days of menstruation. A catamenial pattern is suspected; her menstrual tracking app entries (which she shared in clinic) show temporal association between cycle day and events, with three out of the last four clusters aligning with the perimenstrual window. She also reports that episodes are more likely after long days with disrupted sleep, particularly following early starts for client deadlines, and occasionally after returning from weekends away when routine has been irregular—described by her as “post-journey” days with altered sleep and mealtimes. There have been no generalised tonic–clonic seizures in the past year. No seizure-related injuries. 

Examination: Neurological examination was normal. Vitals within normal limits. 

Investigations: MRI brain (3T, 2023) unremarkable. Routine EEG in 2022 showed left temporal intermittent rhythmic slowing without unequivocal epileptiform discharges. No recent bloods available today. 

Plan: 
- Continue lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily. 
- Continue targeted clobazam 10 mg nocte for 3–5 nights spanning the expected perimenstrual window; she has found this helpful and non-sedating. 
- Obtain trough lamotrigine level, FBC, U&E, LFTs, vitamin D. 
- Maintain seizure and menstrual correlation using her app; we agreed to export a 6‑month report before next review. 
- Discussed sleep regularity and stress management around deadline periods; patient prefers non-pharmacological measures at present. 
- Safety advice reinforced; no restrictions to driving based on current pattern, but she understands to cease driving and inform DVLA if any loss of awareness occurs while driving or if frequency escalates. 
- Review in 6 months, earlier if seizure burden increases or if clusters begin outside the perimenstrual window. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Frequency over the last six months has averaged 1–2 focal impaired-awareness episodes per month.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: "Frequency over the last six months has averaged 1–2 focal impaired-awareness episodes per month." This provides a clear numeric range for current seizure frequency. No other higher frequency is reported; generalized tonic–clonic seizures are absent in the past year. Cluster timing is described but not quantified, so it does not override the monthly rate. Therefore, normalize to '1 to 2 per month.'
seizure_frequency_number: Frequency over the last six months has averaged 1–2 focal impaired-awareness episodes per month.

flaws.description: Gold (1 to 2 per month) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
